basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Fajr — “The Dawn” — opens with five enigmatic oaths upon the dawn, the ten nights, the even and the odd, the passing night, and the soul “endowed with understanding.” Then comes the memory of the peoples who swelled beyond measure — ʿĀd, Thamūd, Pharaoh — and whom their Lord struck with the lash of His chastisement. Then the great mirror of man's two symmetrical errors before his lot: when God showers him with gifts, he believes himself honoured; when God measures out his portion, he believes himself debased. Qushayrī dismantles the double illusion in a sentence: “Debasement is not in poverty; debasement is in abandonment (khidhlān, خِذْلَان) to sin.” And then the sūra runs to its summit — four verses among the most beautiful of the Quran, which close the inner Book of every life: O soul at peace, return to your Lord, well-pleased and well-pleasing — enter then among My servants, and enter My Garden. Qushayrī opens four readings of the nafs muṭmaʾinna, the soul at peace: at peace through knowledge, through remembrance of God, through the announcement of the Garden, or simply: the spirit that has become silent. The soul at peace is the one whose inner noise has fallen still. And it is to her, and to her alone, that God addresses the final invitation.

versets.trad: By the dawn!
versets.commentaire: al-fajr (الفَجْر): the dawn, the bursting forth of light that cleaves the night. The root f-j-r is splendid: it also says the gushing of a spring (infijār al-māʾ), the bursting, what comes out with force. Qushayrī gives the literal senses — the dawn of the sacred month, of Dhū al-Ḥijja, of any day — then opens the inward reading: “It is said: it is the dawn of the hearts of the gnostics, when they rise above mere learning, and the morning of their knowings breaks forth — and then they no longer need the darkness of seeking proofs, because the clarity of evidence has manifested in their hearts.” Outer dawn, inner dawn: the same light, on both scales.

mots.def: fajr: the dawn, the breaking of light; root that also says gushing forth (infijār).

versets.trad: By the ten nights!
versets.commentaire: layālin ʿashr (لَيَالٍ عَشْر): ten nights. Qushayrī offers several identifications, all received by tradition: the first ten nights of Dhū al-Ḥijja, the pilgrimage month and the feast of sacrifice; or the first ten of Muḥarram, whose last is ʿĀshūrāʾ; or the last ten of Ramadan, in which the Night of Qadr is hidden. And one particularly beautiful reading: the ten nights that God speaks of in Moses's story — those that completed the forty of the appointment at Sinai (cf. 7:142: “and We completed it with ten”). The ten nights are then the time one must add to an effort already given for it to become encounter.

versets.trad: By the even and the odd!
versets.commentaire: An inexhaustibly fruitful verse. al-shafʿ (الشَّفْع): the even, the pair; al-watr (الوَتْر): the odd, the singular. Qushayrī unfolds seven successive readings, of which the most beautiful: (1) “Adam was an odd one, then was paired with his wife Eve” — love as passage from the odd to the even. (2) In prayer: some are even (dawn, two units), others odd (sunset, three units). (3) The even is the duality of creaturely attributes (knowledge and ignorance, power and impotence, life and death); the odd is the singleness of God's attributes (knowledge without ignorance, power without impotence, life without death). (4) “The even is intention and will; the odd is the aspiration (himma, هِمَّة) that is not satisfied by the created, and yet cannot reach God without support — so the aspiration remains a stranger.” (5) Most moving: “The even is the ascetic and the worshipper, for each has his peer and counterpart; and the odd is the disciple — as the verse goes: alone among friends in every land — when the goal becomes great, few set out.”

mots.def: shafʿ: the even, what is doubled. Same root as shafāʿa (intercession: to add support).
watr: the odd, the singular; also the name of the odd-numbered supererogatory prayer.

versets.trad: By the night when it passes!
versets.commentaire: yasrī (يَسْرِي): it passes, it journeys — the verb names night travel, the nocturnal journey (isrāʾ, of the same root, names the Prophet's night journey). The night itself is on the move. The oath thus rests not on the still night but on the night in the act of passing — already half spent, already turning toward the dawn. A silent image of this present life: always passing, always already half gone.

mots.def: sarā: to travel by night, to pass on; same root as isrāʾ.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.ar: هَلْ فِي ذَٰلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِّذِي حِجْرٍ
versets.tr: hal fī dhālika qasamun li-dhī ḥijr
versets.trad: Is there not in that an oath for one endowed with understanding?
versets.commentaire: ḥijr (حِجْر) — Qushayrī, in a single word: “lubb (لُبّ), the core, the marrow.” Not merely reasoning intelligence, then, but the inner centre of a being, what is most withheld and most essential in him. The preceding oaths address only this lubb. The sūra presupposes a reader capable of descending into himself and recognising, in the dawn, in the ten nights, in the even and the odd, so many signs addressed to the heart.

mots.def: qasam: oath; same root as aqsama.
ḥijr: reason, understanding; literally “that which holds back”; Qushayrī: the core (lubb).

versets.ar: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ
versets.tr: a-lam tara kayfa faʿala rabbuka bi-ʿād
versets.trad: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with ʿĀd,
versets.commentaire: ʿĀd is the ancient people of southern Arabia to whom the prophet Hūd was sent. The Quran elsewhere recounts their pride and presumption in strength (cf. 41:15: “Who is mightier than us?”). The sūra opens the memory of past peoples not as a history lesson but as a mirror: what your Lord has done, He can do again; and what they have done, one can do again as well.

mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ʿĀd: name of the ancient people to whom Hūd was sent.

versets.trad: and Iram, [the city] of the columns
versets.commentaire: Iram (إِرَم): name of the great city or the tribe, famed for its columns (al-ʿimād, العِمَاد). Early commentators hesitate: a legendary city or a tribe. The next verse will insist: no like was ever created. The image is of a civilisation at its peak, sure of its permanence — and yet now vanished.

mots.def: Iram: proper name, city or tribe of the ʿĀd.
ʿimād: columns, pillars; sign of imposing architecture.

versets.ar: الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُهَا فِي الْبِلَادِ
versets.tr: allatī lam yukhlaq mithluhā fī l-bilād
versets.trad: the like of which was never created in any land,
versets.commentaire: “No like was ever created” — the Quran acknowledges Iram's splendour even as it announces its fall. The greatness of the people is not denied; precisely because it was unmatched, its fall is exemplary. What stands alone in its rank also sometimes stands alone, in the end, to receive the blow.

mots.def: khalaqa in the passive apocopated imperfect.
mots.gram: passive apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: mithl: like, equivalent; with suffix pronoun.
bilād: plural of balad; lands, countries.

versets.trad: and Thamūd who hewed the rocks of the valley
versets.commentaire: jābū (جَابُوا): they hewed, they cut out. Thamūd, the people of the prophet Ṣāliḥ (cf. sūra 91), were famed for their dwellings carved into the rock — one can still see them today at Madāʾin Ṣāliḥ in northwest Arabia. They too had made of stone dwellings they believed indestructible.

mots.def: Thamūd: ancient Arabian people to whom the prophet Ṣāliḥ was sent.
jāba: to dig, to hew, to traverse.

versets.trad: and Pharaoh of the stakes,
versets.commentaire: al-awtād (الأَوْتَاد): stakes, tent-pegs. According to commentators: either the stakes on which Pharaoh tortured those he wished to punish; or, by image, his armies which held him fixed to the ground as pegs hold a tent. A triple witness then: ʿĀd with their columns, Thamūd with their rocks, Pharaoh with his stakes. Three civilisations sure of their anchorage, three anchorages overturned.

mots.def: Firʿawn: proper noun, the Pharaoh of Moses.
awtād: plural of watad, peg, stake; pegs of anchorage.

versets.trad: with all those who proved rebellious in the land
versets.commentaire: ṭaghaw (طَغَوْا): they overflowed, they passed every measure. The verb already met in several earlier sūras (96:6; 91:11). It is the mark of the three peoples named: it was not only evil; it was overflowing beyond the limits God had set within communal life.

mots.def: ṭaghā: to overflow, to pass measure, to revolt against the divine order.

versets.trad: and multiplied corruption therein?
versets.commentaire: al-fasād (الفَسَاد): corruption, decay. In the Quran the word covers both moral corruption and the concrete alteration of things (the land laid waste, the bonds severed, the relations defiled). Aktharū, “they multiplied”: it is not an occasional fault, but a policy of fasād.

mots.def: akthara: to multiply, to make much.

versets.trad: Your Lord poured upon them the lash of chastisement.
versets.commentaire: sawṭ ʿadhāb (سَوْط عَذَاب): the lash of chastisement. The image is harsh — and Qushayrī comments simply: “the severity of the chastisement.” The verb ṣabba, “He poured,” adds the sense of a mass brought down in one stroke, as a liquid is poured out. Upon the three peoples who thought themselves anchored, the chastisement comes as a single motion.

mots.def: ṣabba: to pour, to bring down in one stroke.

versets.trad: Truly, your Lord stands watch.
versets.commentaire: A majestic verse. al-mirṣād (المِرْصَاد): the watchtower, the place from which one observes. Qushayrī, in few words: “Nothing escapes Him.” The verse is not a threat: it is a presence. The Lord is not absent — He is posted. For the one who loves, it is a word of comfort: the Beloved never leaves His watch. For the one who deviates, it is a warning: none passes unseen.

mots.def: mirṣād: watchtower, observation post; noun of place from raṣada (to watch).

versets.ar: فَأَمَّا الْإِنسَانُ إِذَا مَا ابْتَلَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَنَعَّمَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ
versets.tr: fa-ammā l-insānu idhā mā btalāhu rabbuhu fa-akramahu wa-naʿʿamahu fa-yaqūlu rabbī akraman
versets.trad: As for man, when his Lord, to test him, honours him and showers him with blessings, he says: “My Lord has honoured me!”
versets.commentaire: An overwhelming verse. The key word is: idhā mā btalāhu, “when He tests him.” What the verse names a “trial” (balāʾ, بَلاء) is wealth, honour, comfort. The ordinary man does not understand: he takes the gift as a sign of favour and concludes that God loves him because he is well treated. But, says the verse, that too is a trial. The great illusion begins here — confusing divine generosity with a favourable judgment on who one is.

mots.def: fa- + ammā: particle introducing a case.
ibtalā: to test, to try; same root as balāʾ.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.ar: وَأَمَّا إِذَا مَا ابْتَلَاهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَهَانَنِ
versets.tr: wa-ammā idhā mā btalāhu fa-qadara ʿalayhi rizqahu fa-yaqūlu rabbī ahānan
versets.trad: But when, to test him, God measures out His gifts to him, he says: “My Lord has debased me!”
versets.commentaire: The mirror reverses. When God reduces the portion — not when He withdraws it, but when He measures it (qadara), restricts it — man at once concludes: “God has debased me.” Qushayrī then dismantles the double error in a sentence one does not forget: “No. Debasement is not in poverty; debasement is in abandonment to sin (khidhlān li-l-ʿiṣyān, خِذْلَان لِلْعِصْيَان).” It is not the goods received or the goods withheld that measure God's love: it is the inner strengths given, or the heaviness allowed. The true gift is tawfīq (تَوْفِيق, the grace of being turned toward obedience); true debasement is khidhlān, being abandoned to oneself.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: qadara: to measure, to determine; here, to restrict.
ahāna: to debase, to despise; with 1st pers. suffix.

versets.trad: Indeed not! You do not honour the orphan;
versets.commentaire: kallā (كَلَّا): no! Qushayrī: “You yourselves deserve debasement for these blameworthy traits.” The verse is hard, but its logic is limpid: if you wish to speak of honour and debasement, look first at what you do with the orphan — with the most stripped-bare among you. What is done to the smallest reveals the truth of what one is.

mots.def: akrama: to honour; in the plural imperfect.

versets.trad: you do not urge one another to feed the poor;
versets.commentaire: taḥāḍḍūna (تَحَاضُّونَ): reciprocal form — “you stir one another,” “you push one another.” The verse does not say only: “you do not feed the poor”; it says: “you do not urge one another to do it.” It is not only a personal failing, it is the absence of a communal culture of help. No one is saying to the other: come, let us go feed this man.

mots.def: taḥāḍḍa: reciprocal of ḥaḍḍa; to urge, to press one another.
mots.gram: reciprocal imperfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. plur.

versets.trad: you devour the inheritance with avidity;
versets.commentaire: al-turāth (التُّرَاث): the inheritance. aklan lammā (أَكْلًا لَمًّا): Qushayrī, in one word: “violently, brutally.” The word lamm evokes what one swallows without discrimination, sweeping all up. The inheritance in question is in particular the share of the weakest (women, orphans), which the powerful in pre-Islamic times took by violating the customs of distribution.

mots.def: lamm: that which sweeps up all without distinction; avidity.

versets.trad: you love wealth with a love without bounds.
versets.commentaire: jamm (جَمّ): abundant, without measure. The recurring theme (cf. 100:8): love of wealth is not in itself evil — it is boundless love that is. Verses 17-20 draw the portrait of a heart that thinks itself honoured and thinks itself debased, but never measures its true misery.

versets.ar: كَلَّا إِذَا دُكَّتِ الْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا
versets.tr: kallā idhā dukkati l-arḍu dakkan dakkā
versets.trad: Indeed not! When the earth is reduced to dust,
versets.commentaire: dukka (دُكَّتْ): to be reduced to dust, to be crushed. The doubling dakkan dakkā insists: not one blow, but a continuous pounding, level after level, until the earth is nothing but dust. The sūra, which had recalled the fall of ʿĀd, Thamūd, and Pharaoh, now widens the image to the whole earth. All that seemed solid is, in truth, brittle.

mots.def: dakka: to crush, to grind; in the passive perfect feminine.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: intensive reduplication of the verbal noun dakk.

versets.trad: and your Lord comes, with the angels rank upon rank:
versets.commentaire: wa-jāʾa rabbuka, “and your Lord comes.” Qushayrī, careful of the letter, gives two theological glosses: (1) “That is: the angels come at His command.” (2) “He performs an act that He names ‘coming'” — the word “coming” is a human way of speaking for that which, in God, does not pertain to place. God does not move as we move; what comes is His signs, His proofs, His decision made manifest. The angels, themselves, truly come, rank upon rank. The word ṣaff evokes the tight rank, as in prayer.

mots.def: ṣaff: tight rank (as in prayer); distributive reduplication.

versets.ar: وَجِيءَ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِجَهَنَّمَ ۚ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنسَانُ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُ الذِّكْرَىٰ
versets.tr: wa-jīʾa yawmaʾidhin bi-jahannama yawmaʾidhin yatadhakkaru l-insānu wa-annā lahu dh-dhikrā
versets.trad: that Day, Hell shall be brought near; that Day, man will remember... But what will remembering avail him?
versets.commentaire: jīʾa bi-jahannam: “Hell shall be brought.” A striking image: Hell is not a place one goes to — it is brought. Qushayrī reports the tradition: “Hell is drawn forth by seventy thousand reins” (per a ḥadīth of Ibn Masʿūd). And on that Day, man remembers — he understands in retrospect what he should have done. But: annā lahu l-dhikrā, “of what use is remembering to him?”. Qushayrī: “Remembering will be of no profit to him, and no excuse will be accepted.” Remembering in good time is conscience; remembering too late is remorse.

mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: Jahannam: Hell, proper name of the Fire.
tadhakkara: to remember, to call to mind.

versets.trad: He will say: “Woe to me! Would that I had sent forth something for my [true] life!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sums up: “That is: I would have obeyed my Lord, and looked out for myself.” Qaddamtu li-ḥayātī, “I would have sent forth something for my life” — the metaphor is economic: the true life is the one to come; good deeds are advances sent ahead from this life. Man then discovers with horror that he has set nothing aside for the only moment that matters.

mots.def: qaddama: to send forward, to put ahead; perfect 1st pers. sing.

versets.trad: That Day, none chastises as He chastises,
versets.commentaire: The verse permits two readings according to vocalisation. lā yuʿadhdhibu: “none chastises as He chastises” — that is, divine chastisement has no human equivalent. Or lā yuʿadhdhabu (passive): “none is chastised as this one [the wretched] is chastised.” Qushayrī keeps both: the first speaks of the sovereignty of the chastisement; the second, of the singularity of the one who undergoes it.

versets.trad: and none binds with chains as He binds.
versets.commentaire: wathāq (وَثَاق): chain, bond. As with the previous verse, two readings are possible: “none binds as He binds” or “none is bound as this one is bound.” The verse ends the tableau of chastisement and prepares the dazzling contrast of the verses that follow.

mots.def: awthaqa: to chain, to bind firmly.
wathāq: chain, bond; with suffix pronoun.

versets.trad: O soul at peace!
versets.commentaire: Overwhelming verse. al-nafs al-muṭmaʾinna (النَّفْس المُطْمَئِنَّة) — the soul at peace. The word comes from the verb iṭmaʾanna: to grow calm, to settle, to become still as water that no longer trembles. It is the opposite of the agitated soul in debate with itself. Qushayrī gives four readings that illumine one another: (1) “the spirit that has grown calm in the soul” — that is, that has ceased to fight human nature; (2) “at peace through knowledge (maʿrifa, مَعْرِفَة)” — true knowledge produces quietude; (3) “at peace through the remembrance of God (dhikr Allāh, ذِكْر اللَّه)” — constant remembrance gives the heart its seat; (4) “at peace through the announcement of the Garden.” And he adds, in closing: “al-nafs al-muṭmaʾinna, that is the spirit become still (al-rūḥ al-sākina, الرُّوح السَّاكِنَة).” The inner noise has fallen silent. And it is to that soul — not the outwardly pious, not the high-performing — that God calls.

mots.def: muṭmaʾinn: active participle of iṭmaʾanna; to grow calm, to settle, to become still.

versets.trad: Return to your Lord, well-pleased and well-pleasing;
versets.commentaire: irjiʿī (ارْجِعِي): return — in the feminine imperative, for the nafs is feminine in Arabic. And God says to her: return rāḍiya (راضِيَة), “well-pleased (with Him)”, and marḍiyya (مَرْضِيَّة), “well-pleasing (to Him)”. Qushayrī: “content concerning God, accepted by God.” It is the double satisfaction already met in sūra 98 — but here, it is named by God Himself to the soul at peace. The divine word is no longer a promise for the future: it is the encounter itself.

mots.def: rāḍiya: active feminine participle of raḍiya; content, in contentment.
mots.gram: active feminine participle, accusative of state

mots.def: marḍiyya: passive feminine participle; one who has obtained acceptance.
mots.gram: passive feminine participle, accusative of state

versets.trad: enter, then, among My servants,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, in a word: “among My righteous servants (al-ṣāliḥūn, الصَّالِحُون).” The word ʿibādī (عِبَادِي) — My servants — carries the divine possessive: those whom God claims as His own, those He calls His. The soul at peace thus enters a community — not into eternal solitude. The return to God is not an individual withdrawal; it is entry into the company of the friends of God.

mots.def: ʿibād: plural of ʿabd; the servants (of God); with 1st pers. suffix.

versets.trad: and enter My Garden!
versets.commentaire: jannatī (جَنَّتِي): My Garden. Again the divine possessive. It is not the Paradise, it is My Paradise — the place of which God Himself says He is the host. The sūra closes on this double invitation: enter among My servants, enter into My Garden. Company and dwelling. It is the highest point of the Quran on the end of the journey: not extinction, not absorption — but entry into Someone's home.

mots.def: janna: the Garden, Paradise; with 1st pers. suffix.
